description: Monday, 11 May 2026 | Justice Subcommittee A Policing Amendment Bill Hearing of Evidence Submitters: Canterbury Howard League for Penal Reform Cosmo Jeffery, President The Law Association of New Zealand Jacob Parry (TLANZ Criminal Law Committee Member and Technology and Law Committee Member) – Barrister, Mills Lane Chambers Lloyd Gallagher (TLANZ Technology and Law Committee Convenor) – Managing Partner, Gallagher & Co. Consultants Retail NZ Carolyn Young, Retail NZ CEO Denise Garland, Retail NZ Manager, Advocacy Advice & Communications Anjum Rahman Gehan Gunasekara Pipitea Chambers Elizabeth Hall, Co-Chair Te Matakahi, Defence Lawyers Association NZ Te Hunga Roia Maori o Aotearoa Julia Spelman New Zealand Outdoors and Freedom Party Alison Pavlovich, Outdoors and Freedom Party Free Speech Union Hannah Clow, Senior In-house Counsel Su-Wuen Ong Office of the Privacy Commissioner Michael Webster, Privacy Commissioner Jo Hayward, General Counsel Eve Kennedy, Manager Policy Ewan Lincoln, Principal Advisor Policy Lloyd Quartermaine, Senior Communications Advisor
descriptionSummary: Policing Amendment Bill. Canterbury Howard League for Penal Reform. Cosmo Jeffery, President

chunks.text: Yes. Good morning. Yeah, good morning. Now we've got- Kia ora... 10 minutes with you. What's that? Okay, so we start, right? Yes, please. Okay. So, kia ora, everybody. My name is Cosmo Jeffrey. I'm currently the president of the Canterbury Howard League for Penal Reform, and with me today is Buster Thompson, our secretary.

As we stated in our written submission, the Howard League has been advocating for penal reform for over 100 years. While we primarily engage in advocating for reform related to prisons, we also pride ourselves on promoting an evidence-based consideration of all proposed legislation that relates to justice and civil liberty.

We are aware that there are other organizations who provided much more detailed submissions on this topic, and the Howard League would like to support those organizations in submitting that we oppose the needless expansion of police powers. We believe we should be strengthening privacy laws rather than weakening them.

More especially when it was revealed that over 50% of those images taken by the police were of persons of Maori and Pasifika descent. As an organization, the Howard League is confronted daily by the inherent discrimination that is rife within our justice system.

Polynesian and Maori people are totally over-represented in all the cohorts we advocate for the New Zealand justice system, and the police are no exception. More Maori and Pasifika people are stopped, apprehended, arrested, tried in our courts, and imprisoned by a factor of at least three times compared to Pakeha subjects.

Considering the disproportionate impact this will have on Maori, we think that more effort is required to work with Maori and Maori organizations in developing legislation related to the collection and storage of information. Consequently, the Howard League urges the Justice Committee to take a breath and consider fully the implications of this legislation.

In preparing our submission, we've basically decided that it's easy to criticize. Anyone can criticize, but what we want to do is try and provide some answers. So we researched what other European jurisdictions do in basically managing public surveillance and storage of collected data, and what we found was really interesting.

Most of the countries in the European Union have signed up, in fact, all of them, as far as we can work out, have signed up to the GDPR. Hmm. I didn't know what this was either, but it's the General Data Protection Regulation. So they all sign up to the General Data Protection Regulation, and this sets strict regulations which control the surveillance and storage of people in public places.

For example, there's a proportionality requirement. Police cannot indiscriminately photograph the entire population. The collection of biometric data is prohibited in principle, but if permitted, if it's strictly necessary, it must be proportionate for investigating specific crimes or maintaining security.

The storage and retainment. The EU allows the retention of biometric data of suspects and convicts, but national laws must set appropriate time limits for the reviewing and the need to store that data. For example, in most European countries, if any of the people whose images are taken and stored do not offend in the next 12 months, that data is deleted, and that's what we believe should happen in New Zealand.

We could outline this in much greater detail, but to be truthful, the parliamentary committee, the Justice Committee, and you guys generally, have much more efficient and capable research people who can find this information. We don't need to reinvent the wheel.

There are basically instructions and institutions in place that deal with this sort of thing. Why should we be starting out from scratch and having a carte blanche system where people can be willingly recorded and data stored infinitely? We believe that a body such as the Privacy Commissioner should be set up to oversee this legislation and be responsible for the taking and storage of images in the public.

If those people do not offend for the next 12 months, then that data should be deleted. That makes sense. There needs to be a clear purpose for why police are taking photos of someone rather than a vague possible future purpose. Otherwise, effectively, anyone's photo can be taken by the police indiscriminately and stored forever. We believe this not to be a fair and reasonable situation, and we want it to be controlled.Um- All right... that's our oral submission, and we could answer any questions if there were any.

Hey, well, thank you very much. Honorable Dr. Duncan Webb. Uh, kia ora, and kia ora Cosmo and Buster. Thanks for all the work that you do. Look, I guess, most people would accept that if police are investigating a crime or, uh, something they know is planned as a crime, that surveillance and photographs is probably justifiable.

Do you have any comments on them taking photographs, where they are really just gathering information about the behaviors of people that they know are part of criminal networks rather than investigating a particular crime or proposed crime?

Well, our view is that it goes, it should be for a particular purpose, in that it should be to investigate a crime rather than just... Otherwise, you get this risk of investigating people who they consider associates but are not actually committing any offenses. I think it goes too far to be investigating or taking photos, this biometric information of people who are not actively involved in a crime.

Thanks, Flip. Um, just personally, I don't know if this helps, but it's no secret that I was, I have been in prison. Uh, it was over 25 years ago. I was down in Twizel the other day, and I got stopped by the police on my Ducati, and he asked my license. Within seconds, he brought up my history, started talking about something that happened 25 years ago, and I was left standing there wondering, "Haven't I done the time? Shouldn't I be allowed to get on with my life?"

And I'm just wary of collecting information on people that can be called up within seconds and used against them when it doesn't relate to the whole idea of taking images of people. All right. Hey, well, thank you for sharing that, particularly your last personal comment, which was very interesting.

Um, and both of you this morning joining us on this submission, so thanks for your time. Right. Now, we're going to go to the New Zealand- No other questions, I take it? No. Um- Thank you. Yeah. Thank you. Thank you for your time. Thank you very much, Cosmo. Thank you. Um, right. We're going to go to the Law Association of New Zealand.

Uh, and hopefully we've got a couple of people. Jacob Perry, Perry's there, I see, and maybe Lloyd Gallagher. If you're free, please join us. Oh, do we have Lloyd? Yeah. Uh, Lloyd is there, but he's on mute. All right. Well, that's all right. I can, uh, I can speak on Lloyd's behalf.

All right. Um, tena kouto- Good to have you. Now we've got 10 minutes with you, so please leave a little bit of time for questions. Thank you. Thank you. Tena kouto. Good morning. Um, so yes, I'm Jacob Perry. Uh, appearing with me is Mr. Lloyd Gallagher. Um, we're appearing on behalf of the Law Association's Technology and Law Committee. Um, and thank you for giving us the opportunity to appear this morning. Um, I won't retread the written submissions that we have filed, but this morning I just wanted to touch on, uh, three points. Um, firstly,

uh, whether or not this bill, uh, as it is, directly responds to the issue which it sets out to address. Um, I want to make some comments on the scope and threshold provisions, uh, in the bill, and then also, uh, some general comments about the, uh, technological context in which this is occurring.

Um, and I suppose at the outset, I just want to be clear that, uh... or be clear that we acknowledge the underlying legislative objective here. So the Supreme Court decision in Tamifuna, uh, and before that, uh, the IPCA, uh, Privacy Commissioner joint inquiry, uh, there is uncertainty arising out of that, and there is a need for, uh, a clear basis for information collection.

What we want to ensure is that in addressing this issue, we don't end up with, uh, either further uncertainty, uh, or that, or finding that we address the issue in a way that, uh, is inconsistent with, um, individuals' reasonable expectations of privacy. So, um, the first point I wanted to make was just linking this back to where we've come from, uh, with the Supreme Court decision in Tamifuna. Uh, now that decision didn't simply say police can't take photos in public.

Um, it turns on a lot more than that. Um, there's the taking the image, identifying the person, retaining the image for intelligence use without any, uh, defined purpose, uh, and without any, uh, framework behind it for retention or oversight, destruction, um, et cetera.

Um, the Cabinet paper which preceded this bill, um, specifically referenced the judgment of Justice Koch in Tamifuna. Um, and in that judgment, His Honor said, uh, that what was needed, uh, was obviously, uh, uh, some sort of statutory authority for this, but also, uh, a clear framework for retention, use, and destruction. Uh, and there was a comment that the limits and the broad framework for any such database should be determined by Parliament, by amendment of the Policing Act.

So the bill that we have now, uh, inserts collection powers, but it doesn't do those other things. It doesn't give us a framework, um, after that for retention and use and destruction. And that's the really, really important part here, which I think with the bill is a structural problem. Um, the importance when it comes to privacy rights, the importance is the use.

Um, I think initially, uh-In relation to this bill, it was anticipated that we would account for this by including some sort of regulation-making power. But that doesn't seem to have carried through to the bill itself, and I'm not 100% sure why that is. But that could have been one of the ways in which this was addressed.

So that's point number one from our submissions, is that some thought needs to go into what the framework after collection of data looks like and how that's going to be implemented. So if there's authority to collect it, what restrictions are there going to be on its further use? The second point that I wanted to address, which I think is set out in more detail in our written submissions, but just the threshold and scope of collection powers that we're dealing with here are broad. So, we have section 45A, which gives us our purposes, some specified

purposes, but also any other lawful purpose connected with functions or activities of the police. Now, when you pair that with the threshold in Section 45D, because this purpose is open-ended, our concern is that it leads to a risk of indiscriminate collection without a clearly defined purpose, which is the same concern that was being echoed in Tamifuna, and before that, the joint inquiry.

45D, which I just referenced, sets the threshold for intelligence collection. And at the moment, it is or it could be used in a vague way. So the threshold is information will or may support police functions or activities. So, just on its face, that could support or, sorry, it could permit collection on a purely speculative basis.

So, well, this might be useful in the future, but I don't have any real reason to collect it right now. So there's a threshold issue in our view. And the third point I wanted to make on the provisions was just about continuous recording of sound and video solely for intelligence purposes.

Now, in the bill, this is subject to a reasonableness test, but I do note that in the cabinet paper, that continuous recording for intelligence purposes was not intended to be included. And it's unclear why the bill has, I suppose, taken a different direction.

So our submission well, on that at least, if there is going to be an authority for continuous intelligence only recording, there needs to be a framework around that. So what sort of duration medium is anticipated? Who can authorize this? Is this going to be reviewed?

How long can it be held? These sorts of things. I realize I'm whipping through my time here, but so the third point that I'll make is I think more of a general one, which has to do with the technological context in which all of this is occurring. So, not just police technology, all technology in New Zealand and globally at the moment, is enabling a greater use of mass data, and it's moving at quite a fast pace.

If we are introducing broad collection powers without a framework that governs future use of data and retention, the practical effect is we're going to vastly expand the volume of information being put into databases, and we may not currently know, or even anticipate the use that it's going to be put to.

There was a comment that came from the Supreme Court in Tamifuna that advances in technology may transform the practical effect of otherwise familiar police practices. So intelligence notings are familiar, commonplace police activity. But the modern version of that which we seem to be looking at is including much larger, massive amounts of personal information being pushed into police databases and the ability to use and analyze it at scale, that is not clear to us at present.

So that's the overall point, being, I think, how this data is going to be used. Actually, I could stop there. Yes. We've got Dr Laurence Sunan, got a question for you. Thanks, Chair. Kia ora, Jacob, can you hear me? Yes. Cool. Just on the data front, I'm really interested to know also if you've done any work around how that data can be shared with agencies overseas. Are you familiar with Migrant Fife?

I'm not. So- I don't know if Lloyd might Yeah, I'll jump in on this one a little bit- Yeah... if that's all right So just going from there, can you see potentially data like this being used by other countries? Yeah. Look, we've already seen some of the work that I'm doing with Curtin University, and my PhD around AI and the risks of AI as well, is we've already seen the risks of data being able to be collated, and compared, and then fed between an AI to another AI, and simply data breach risks that are around that.

The amount of data that we're looking to collect here and the general risk for public informationUm, that is required under different Official Information Acts and all those sort of acts, uh, as well as the confidentiality to require some of it to be withheld. There is always a general risk of how that data's going to be shared and released in long-term, uh, ventures, especially with the technology and how it's developing.

One of the other risks is with the collaboration that we're now doing with the FBI, et cetera, internationally, we run a risk of that information no longer being able to be within our control. Uh, once it's in the US's hands, under the Patriot Act, et cetera, there's a massive amount of risk of that data being put into databases and miscommunicated along different areas.

We saw the new AI acts that are coming in that have sort of been floated around America at the moment, where one agency is able to provide that information to another agency. Microsoft is able to use it for other purposes under the terms and conditions. Um, it, it creates a minefield for potential risk and, uh, inappropriate use of this information and, and that's why we think there, there should be limits on it.

Right. Um, Honorable Dr. Duncan Webb. Thanks for that. Look, um, little less technical than Lawrence, but look, I'm interested in whether you think in terms of legitimate collection of information, there is a tenable way to frame intelligence gathering rather than investigation. Um, because it seems to be one of the things that we're really struggling with. We all accept that gathering data for investigations is one thing, but it's this loose kind of concept of being able to go about and collect intelligence, which

seems to be hard to pin down. Well, that's one of the risks that we've always had, is that the intelligence side of things is always about the potential for misuse of, uh, of behavior in the future. So the, we have a whole security system and Securities Act around collecting that sort of intelligence. Uh, then we're, we're talking about generally collecting ordinary people's information and the general public.

They're not quite the s- and, and in my opinion, they're not quite the same thing because what we do for intelligence, uh, collection really has a, a lot of frameworks around that and a, and a bit more broader approach. Whereas what we talked about in general public, when you're walking down the street is more around the protection of an individual where there is no potential crime, but may be one in the future. So this, this comes back to the same sort of arguments we had with DNA testing in the early years, where how long do you keep

DNA information? Is it for a potential, uh, rape or abuse in the future? And where should the limits on that information be? And, and rightly pointed out by some others, GDPR does put some limitations on that, and I, and in my opinion, I think we should follow that same sort of approach because it does create that better balance.

Um, Jacob, did you have anything to add to that? No, no. I, I just think that, um, e- even if, um, even if we're talking about general intelligence purposes, um, I, I think we have to start, um, from a place where we have a framework around h- you know, how, how long or for what purposes it's going to be used, uh, data's going to be used for.

Um, uh, uh, and that's really the big problem with the bill at the moment, uh, is that it's t- it's just, it's left a big blank space in there. And, and it is a tricky question. I, I acknowledge the que- uh, acknowledge the question, but, um, the answer to that question isn't leaving the answer blank. All right. Hey guys, we've gone over time, but, um, very good, uh, submission and, um, drew out some points that we haven't heard before.

So thank you for both of you for coming on this morning. You're very welcome. Thank you for having us. Right. Now I'm going to-- Thank you. We're going to Carolyn Young from the Retail New Zealand. Hopefully Carolyn's, uh... Good morning. You've turned up in per- person and we're not there with you this morning. Hey, good. Hey, lovely to see you again.

Um, looking forward to hearing what you've got to say. Thank you very much. Um, and just introduce my colleague. I'm Denise Farland. I'm the advocacy manager for Retail NZ. Look, thank you very much for, um, the time to chat this morning. We, in principle, um, support the bill. Um, what, there is a small exception, as has been heard in the previous two submissions.

What we'd say in terms of retailers, that the majority of New Zealanders would be walking through a retail store at least once a week or once a fortnight across New Zealand. Uh, we have a high degree of recidivist offending in retail where, um, people are coming back again and again and again, and we know that retail crime is a significant problem.

We wanna ensure that police have the right tools in order to, uh, be able to capture offenders, and then obviously send them through the justice system in a way in which they'll get charged and, uh, in a way that will be meaningful. But we do agree with the prior two submissions that, um, you know, there needs to, we need to ensure that there's the right controls that are sitting in place, uh, for, you know, the, the framework of any collection of data. We sit here believing that the police do a really great job and that they are collecting information for the right reasons.

Uh, we would start at a, um, not at the lowest common denominator space, but starting to say that on the position that we believe they do the right work. We need to give them the right tools, there needs to be the right framework, and have the right protections, including, you know, they need to comply to privacy as much as anybody else, and proportionality also needs to comply. Um, you know, and we would think, uh, smarter people than us would be able to look into what's o- overseas that we could learn from.

You know, they did talk about the general data protection regulation in Europe. You know, maybe that's something that someone could look into, but, but as long as we've got the right framework and protections to ensure that people are protected, we believe that police need the tools to do the job. Um, they would be the key things from our perspective. And what about, uh, the expanding the road closures? You're happy with all that?

The road closures. Yes. The expansion of the road closure powers, we think, again, police need to be able to have the right tools in place to be able to keep our communities safe from harm. And whether that is in terms of being able to give out fines and have the correct penalties in place to ensure that police can close off roads when required and stop people from going into those areas when it is not a safe environment for them to be in. We're generally supportive a- again of that.

And I'll just add, just in a retail environment, we're seeing guns presented at store these days. So it's not just machetes and knives and other weapons, but we're seeing people turn up with guns. And when those situations happen, we get quick response from police. They need to be able to cordon off areas and make sure that no one else comes into those public spaces because retail environments are a sort of a pseudo-public space where you've generally got free entry into.

So having those controls to close off areas to ensure that our communities are safe are really important. All right, good. Right, members, questions. Just in terms of your most retailers, in terms of their storage, I see that you support police being able to record in private premises such as a retail store. So I presume that's what you mean by that.

What's the norm for retailers in terms of collecting images, particularly video images in a shop or whatever? Is there a norm around that? I would say 99% of retailers have CCTV, and there's wide use of CCTV across retail. Multiple cameras throughout store. People should be checking those things.

And they're compliant to legislation around how long they can hold onto that information for. And police being able to come back into store, I for instance, happen to be able to review tape is important. It's something like a 30 or 60 day, something like that. I don't actually have that time on the tip of my tongue, I'm sorry. But it is a much longer period of time than, say, for example, facial recognition where, if a retailer's using that, it's deleted within a minute of it being collected.

Okay. Right. Camilla Village. Yeah, just a sorry, a follow-up supplementary to the chair's question. I just wanted to know, obviously I understand the vast majority of retailers will be very compliant with providing the CCTV footage. But in those instances where that isn't available in the store or for some reason not made available, this bill may allow the ongoing surveillance outside a retail store, even if that is considered a private place, as that's visible publicly.

And obviously with retail stores, that might be more likely to be visible publicly than in other private places. And so is that something that you've consulted any of your members on in terms of your support for that ongoing surveillance, or is it more of the provision of CCTV so you wouldn't imagine that would be utilized in many instances?

There's probably two answers to that question. The first piece I'll go with in terms of member feedback, look, we've had members come to us and we had a with Privacy Commissioner and with the Police Commissioner and have asked them, can we share or can police share their watchlist or some of their people that they're most worried about with the retailers' watch lists if they've got FRT so that we can make sure we can keep our stores safe. So some retailers are really open about being able to have police insert data into their space.

Obviously at the moment you can't do that. But I would also expect as a second point would be that we would, if police are wanting to put in place, I guess you'd call it surveillance for want of a better word, we would expect that there would be adequate reasoning that sits in behind that and purpose for it. So we're not just surveilling the whole country for anything, but there's actual specific reasons to understand why someone would be coming to that space on a regular basis and is undertaking something that would be

viewed to be illegal and will lead them to prosecution down the line. So there would have to be something substantive to it. All right. Hey, thank you Carolyn and Denise for your presentation. Nice to see you again. Nice to see you. Thanks. Right. Now, we've got Anjum Rehman, who I see you are there. As-salaam alaikum.

Good morning. We've got five minutes with you. Wa alaikum as-salaam. Tēnā koutou katoa. Bismillahirrahmanirrahim. I'm presenting not as an expert, but just as a member of the public, and in my written submission I talked about the oversurveillance of our communities, which was a fact up to at least May 2018, per the Royal Commission of Inquiry report on the Christchurch mosque terrorist attacks.

And, in this instance, and for some of the powers in this bill, what we're looking at is young Maori men who were the target of oversurveillance. And oversurveillance is part of over-securitization and is not the most effective way to reduce crime.

We saw after the Christchurch mosque attacks, securitization of mosques with surveillance cameras and various other measures in place. But I don't believe that is what would make the mosques safer. If anyone was so inclined, they'd just kill people as they came out onto the street.Do we then securitize the whole of society?

In the US, if we take that as an example, where they have so much securitization of shops, glass fronts, you can't interact with people directly. The things that are happening in schools for their kids, having to do drills and all these kinds of things, but it hasn't really resulted in lower gun-related deaths. So what I'm saying is that prevention efforts are much more effective and better worth our resources, and I do want to acknowledge the support of the New Zealand Police after the Christchurch mosque attacks, and that my submissions

are presented with the utmost respect for that support. And on the same theme of over-surveillance and securitization, this bill potentially removes the right to political protest, and the Law Commission have laid out their concerns in this regard. And if we look at what is happening overseas, our concerns or my concerns are that this kind of thing could start to happen here. So for example, in the UK, we're seeing the criminalization of elderly pacifists through the use of terrorism laws in incidences where there's no lives were lost.

We've seen overriding of basic protection. So for example, some of those people that have been tried around damaging military assets, the jury that has been making decisions are not allowed to know that if they convict them or if they find them guilty, that they will be sentenced under terrorism laws and not just laws related to vandalism of property. This kind of overreach is incredibly scary, and that's what slippery slopes are about. When we look at what's happening across

the ditch in Australia and the banning in one of their states of the term global intifada, and we're not seeing the defenders of free speech coming out, protecting against the criminalization of a term of protest against theft of land, loss of life, access to health, education, and more.

Within our context, yes, there's a particular religious group that comes out to harass other religious communities and other communities that protest, who misuse the haka. They always target minority groups and not the majority, and I want to offer my solidarity to the Sikh community who were their latest target.

The answer is not to change the law in a way that could stifle political protest against state terrorism, against the killing of tens of thousands of children, against illegal wars when there was never an imminent threat. And I could be talking about the illegal invasion of Iraq more than 20 years ago or the illegal bombing of Iranian citizens and schoolgirls this year.

Discrimination and other previous speakers have talked about automated decision-making, AI algorithms, machine learning. And as AI is increasingly used in state agencies, there is not enough scrutiny, and there is no effective regulation of how that data is going to be used and the ongoing effect.

But I had more to say, but I think other speakers have covered that much better than I could. And so the powers in this bill are going to make that worse. And in this space and around AI and platform regulation, there is a lack of effective regulation overall as it is, and what protections there are, are now starting to be dismantled, and we see that with the BSA being taken away. But even if there was no discrimination, the collection and holding of data by the state in this manner with so little safeguard is an overreach. And

investing in adequate food, secure housing, and warm housing, education, health, social cohesion, all of that would solve these problems so much more effectively than what is proposed in this bill. Thank you. All right. Hey, thank you for your submission.

We're well over time, but I didn't want to interrupt you, because I thought what you had to say was very important. But unfortunately, we'll have to move on. But thank you for coming on this morning and making your thoughts known to the committee. Right. Now, we're going to Professor Gunesekara. I think I saw you are on.
If you'd like to join us, that would be fantastic. We've got 10 minutes with you. Professor, are you available? I think you've got your mute on.

There we are. Good morning, Professor. Yes, good morning, Mr. Chair. Thank you for the opportunity to present on this in a personal capacity. I just want to clarify that my role, I'm an academic at the University of Auckland. I have for decades been researching and teaching and publishing in privacy law.

But I'm also a past chair and currently the co-convener of the Privacy Foundation's surveillance... Sorry, I'll just start my video. I just realized. Apologies for that. Yes, so, I'm the co-convener of the Privacy Foundation's surveillance working group.

However, I'm not speaking to you this morning in that role. I think the foundation is making a submission on its own account, so I'm speaking purely as a privacy observer and a commentator and academic. And for the record, I completely support the Privacy Foundation's position. We have completely opposed the bill.

We've taken a, in principle, view that we oppose the bill, and that is also my position. However, I want to address, and I oppose both Part 1 and Part 2, but I want to speak specifically to some specifics of Part 1 on the basis that, if it does go ahead, then I would like to see some specific safeguards. And these relate to things like clarifying the relationship.

They're largely of a technical nature, clarifying the relationship between the bill, the amendment bill, and the Privacy Act, and the Biometric Processing Privacy Code. But I want to start this morning by dispelling the notion there's a public, that people might have the idea that somehow if the police take pictures in public places, and even on private property, it's the same as you or me taking a photo of members of the public. There's a huge difference between you or I taking a photo, and the police, because the police then retain that

picture, that photo, that image, goes into a database, and it can be used in a way, perhaps months or years later, that has serious effects on the freedom of that individual concerned. Whereas if you and I take a photo of somebody, we don't have the power to later on go and arrest them or detain them or prevent them getting a job if there's a police vetting inquiry.

So these are the kinds of distinctions that I think need to be made. There's also the concern of what I see as mission creep, and I think some of your previous speakers have hinted at this. I have also heard rumors about the Migration Five, and I'm currently working, in fact, I have written a piece on this, happy to make that available to the committee, on the proposal by the current consultation with the border protection authorities, with Homeland Security in the United States, where to get our visa waiver status, the United States is

insisting on direct access to other countries' law enforcement databases. And that is, to me, very concerning. I see there's a question from- No, you finish, please, Professor Yeah. So, that is a concern. And we have examples previously of American companies like Clearview.

Once they get access to these images, they go in public databases, and it's very difficult to then get them deleted. I want to speak very quickly about the difference between biometric information, which this bill authorizes, and other forms of biographical information. There is a big difference. In the age of AI and in the age of facial recognition, images are very different from a police constable taking a note in his or her notebook.

Images can identify people instantly, be matched, be stored in databases, and have a much more significant potential. Your face is your face. You cannot really get a new face if somebody hacks into it. And there's no guarantee for the police, or anybody for that matter, that these images won't end up, for example, on the dark web or eventually, if there's a hack or something like that.

But I want to also talk about the overwide definitions such as policing intelligence, intelligence or other lawful purposes, integrity of policing. These purposes are currently defined very broadly in Section 9 of the act, and they are so broad as to potentially include any of these kinds of use that the police find for the information.

So that's the mission creep that I'm talking about. That could actually be counterproductive because community support really means taking the community with you, not

necessarily making them the objects of surveillance. So that was an issue. The other issue I wanted to raise is to dispel the notion that somehow all we're doing is restoring the status quo. I've listed these points in my submission. There are sections of the Policing Act at the moment, sections 32, 33, and 34 of the Principal Act, which allow the police to retain images and other biographical data.

And there are various conditions on that, that that information is for current proceedings or when someone is detained. And this bill changes that status quo by now allowing those images to be retained, potentially indefinitely, as I think one of your previous speakers also mentioned.

There is the issue identified by the Independent Police Complaints Authority and the OPC joint investigation about access and correction to those records, and the police previously have struggled to actually-- I know they have made some improvements, and I have a great deal of respect for the police. They have been trying hard to improve their data collection and storage practices.

We have no right of deletion in New Zealand, and that is a great concern to me as a privacy academic. I think our law needs to have retention limits, and that's something that's currently not in the law. And in conclusion, I also think if this bill goes ahead, there needs to be some provision for not only very clear statements that the Privacy Act needs to continue to apply, but also the need for subsequent powers of audit for the Privacy Commissioner or the Independent Police Complaints Authority to be able to audit how these new collection powers are being used, and that they're not being used in an over-broad way. What this bill does is remove the existing requirement under our privacy law that information gathering needs to be necessary for a lawful purpose.

The change is subtle in wording but enormous in consequence. It means the threshold for collecting information is now so low as to be non-existent. This is not a bill to assist targeted policing. This is a bill to introduce routine surveillance of New Zealanders.

The privacy commissioner has warned that these powers could have a chilling effect on civil rights and freedoms. That warning deserves serious attention. When people believe they may be photographed, monitored, and permanently recorded by the state without suspicion of wrongdoing, behavior changes. That's not hypothetical.

And history tells us that when broad surveillance powers are expanded, they continue to expand in practice over time. What that demonstrates to us is that where legislation is without detail, where legislation doesn't build in safeguards and necessary constraint, left to their own devices, the New Zealand police, on occasion, make errors of judgment that affect New Zealand citizens.

So a good law is not built on trust and good intentions. It's built on clear limits, accountability, transparency, and democratic legitimacy. This bill fails on every demand that we would have of it. Transparency is essential when the state is granted extraordinary power. This debate is not about whether the police should be able to investigate crime. Of course, they should.

The public expects and demands the police keep communities safe and have effective tools to do so. But effective policing and civil liberties are not opposing values. They depend on each other. Public trust is one of the most important tools policing has. This trust is strengthened when powers are necessary, proportionate, transparent, and subject to meaningful oversight.

Te Matakāhi, the Defense Lawyers' Association, and the lawyers appearing before you are not saying do nothing. We are saying slow down, consult properly, define powers clearly, and ensure that laws that are made reflect modern democratic expectations of privacy and accountability.

We are not being obstructive. We are being responsible. We do not want laws that dramatically change the fabric of our society because of a knee-jerk rebellion to a Supreme Court judgmentOnce powers of surveillance and control are normalized, they will not be rolled back.

So the decisions that the select committee make, and the parliament make, will define the relationship between the state and the citizens of New Zealand for years to come. And what we say is that that relationship should be grounded in rights, trust, and democratic principle.

Mr. Webster, looking forward to hearing from you. We've got a reasonable amount of time that's been set aside as you gather your team. So, I'll leave it to you to introduce your team. We've got until 12:45, we'll just see how we go. Thank you, Mr. Chair.

Can I introduce my colleagues, on my left, Jo Haywood, who's our general counsel. On my right, Ewan Lincoln, who's our principal advisor policy. I also have my manager of policy, Ian Kennedy, with me today as well. A number of us are lawyers, some of us aren't.

Very good. Mr. Chair, members, as you will have seen from my written submission, I do not support part one of the bill, and I recommend that it not be passed. It's not often that I call for a legislative proposal to be completely rejected, and I do so now because I have deep concerns about the implications with part one.

Some context that may be of interest to the committee. My office has just completed its 2026 privacy survey, and the results suggest that our fellow Kiwis are increasingly concerned about their right to privacy and what happens with their personal information, such as an image captured by photo or video.

Some results, Mr. Chair. Over half say they have become more concerned about privacy in recent years. 82%, an extraordinarily high figure, want more say in how their information is collected and used. And confidence that New Zealand law adequately protects personal information is low. Only 18% are extremely or very confident, while a third have little or no confidence at all that New Zealand law adequately protects their personal information.

So, in today's more fractious times, trust and confidence in important state institutions, like the police, is a precious thing, and I am concerned that the proposals in part one of the bill risk undermining that trust and confidence. It appears that under this provision, information may be collected simply because it may, in some unspecified way, support the police in carrying out its functions and activities.

As an indication of the potential breadth of this authorization, the regulatory impact statement for the bill refers to collection for general intelligence purposes in terms of collection for an unknown or unspecified future policing purpose and a known or unknown future lawful use.

By providing such a broad authorization for police collection of information, part one of the bill creates significant privacy risks that could lead to police collecting and retaining information about New Zealanders that police have no good reason to hold. I believe the bill needs to include an objective, reasonable threshold for police collection of information.

Mr. Chair, members, no compelling case has been made for police to receive such wide powers to collect people's information. My office has asked for examples of specific scenarios in which police considers that the current legal settings prevent its officers from effectively performing their functions, and we have not been provided with any such examples.

I understand that police want legal certainty, but I don't believe the bill as drafted provides that certainty. Because the boundaries of the police's powers are unclear in this bill, they are likely to be tested in court. The courts will still be able to determine whether police actions constitute an unreasonable search in the circumstances.The broad authorisation in this bill also means that important protections in the Privacy Act may not apply. In fact, will not apply.

I do not accept that police should have such a broad discretion to collect, use, and retain personal information without reasonable limits being in place. At the same time, the bill lacks safeguards and provides no certainty to New Zealanders that their privacy rights will be protected.

I am concerned about how this bill impacts on people's civil and political rights. For example, it's possible that the bill could allow police to take video of a group of young people, say, hanging out in a park, not because they are connected to a possible crime, a possible investigation, or a public safety reason, but simply to keep tabs on a group of people because the video might be useful one day.

I'm not saying that police would necessarily take video in those situations, but I believe the bill would allow them to do so, so long as the officers can loosely connect the photos to a policing function or activity. And once the photos have been taken, they can be kept, of course, on police's systems, potentially indefinitely, even though the people in the photos may have no connection to criminal activity.

On that, I'm also concerned that police has still not addressed the serious inadequacies in its management and oversight of information through necessary improvements to its own information management systems, a problem that they themselves have identified. As a result, therefore, I have concerns around the storage, the retention, and destruction, and indeed, the secondary use of personal information conducted and collected under the powers in this bill.

In place of part one of this bill, I would like to see a more careful and a more considered process to develop a legislative framework for police intelligence gathering. I believe that such a framework could strike an appropriate balance between protecting privacy and allowing police to collect the information it needs.

However, if the bill proceeds, it is essential, I believe, that it be amended to provide protections for people's privacy and their rights. I'd like to see the effect of the privacy principles in the Privacy Act maintained, other safeguards included in the bill, either through primary or secondary legislation, safeguards being subject to independent oversight, and the commencement date for the bill pushed back to allow safeguards to be put in place.

Key safeguards should include controls on retention and secondary use of personal information, requirements for record-keeping, accountability, and transparency about the exercise of police powers. And as I noted in my submission, if I can assist the committee in developing appropriate safeguards, I would be happy to provide further advice or to appear before the committee again.

In conclusion, Mr. Chair, members, the police, of course, do have an important and challenging job to do. But given their significant coercive powers, they need to do and be seen to do that job in a way that protects and respects law-abiding New Zealanders'

rights to privacy, no matter where they are. Sweeping police powers may inevitably catch some criminals but are inconsistent with life in a free and democratic society. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Thank you, members. Right. Thank you very much. We'll go to Ginny Andersen first.

Thank you. Thank you very much. Thank you for your submission. It was very thorough. I have two questions. The first is that the Minister of Police has stated that this legislation simply reinstates the law as it was before the Tamifuna case. Is that your view? And if not, why? I believe that in the case of Tamifuna, that was a specific response by the Supreme Court to a specific situation. I don't think that the police has lost anything from, for example, the work that we did through our IPCA OPC inquiry into the photograph

of a youth in the Wairarapa or indeed the Tamifuna decision. Jo, would you like to talk a bit more about Tamifuna? Yes. Thank you, Michael. Yes, I think the Commissioner's submission departs from the police's view that this bill is simply restoring the law.

Our perspective is that the joint report and Tamifuna were highlighting the legal constraints applying to police when they are conducting this activity. So rather than narrowing the law, police carry out their powers within a broader statutory framework. That framework includes the Policing Act principles, the Search and Surveillance Act, New Zealand Bill of Rights Act, the Land Transport Act, and the Privacy Act.

So the report by the OPC and the IPCA and the Supreme Court's decision were applying the statutory framework to the particular factual situations, and in Mr. Tamifuna's case, that involved a traffic stop under the Land Transport Act. Thank you. Thank you very much. My second question is in relation to your comments around the storage and retention of digital images.

We've asked a number of questions about this going forward, and I was still not reassured that there was sufficient police IT infrastructure or the requisite funding to enable the internal systems to be able to record evidence and hold it in such a way that it would be able to be checkedThe only answer that we did get was that the new electronic notebook that police used would be of assistance to be able to do this. But I still didn't have the reassurance that there was the sufficient investment into IT infrastructures to enable some of the checks and balances that

you have outlined in your submission. Is there anything that you would like to add to that in terms of your awareness of the capability of police to be able to monitor and retain and destroy images? Yes. Thank you for that. The police certainly have a number of tools to take digital images. The issue for us, and that's been brought into sharp relief through the response to our formal compliance notice under the Privacy Act, in relation to the taking of photographs in the Wairarapa, is the ability to store those in a way that they can be readily retrieved or indeed,

deleted when they're not needed anymore. We have, through that compliance notice, been seeking to have all unlawfully taken photographs deleted from police systems, and that is one area where police have told us, and in fact have said publicly, that they have been unable to identify those photos due to inadequacies in their current information management systems. So yes, there are some significant challenges there.

What we will inevitably see under the proposal set out in the bill is the taking of photographs of people who have been going about their lawful everyday business. Those photos are going to end up in a police system next to people who have been convicted or accused of some of our most worst crimes.

It's all together in one database. And the ability for that material to be retrieved, I think, is an open question, given the current inadequacies in the way the systems are working at present. And just one last question, please, Mr. Chair. Do you think that introducing this legislation is premature, given that the required IT infrastructure to do what it is required through privacy is currently not in place?

So, two things. One, I would say if this bill proceeds as it is, and I would certainly hope that if it was the view of the government to proceed with this legislation, that they would consider the natures of the amendments we've proposed. There would need to be a much greater period set aside before it comes into force in terms of commencement, to enable police to put in place the necessary systems, procedures, and infrastructure to enable it to function effectively. Second, though, my first best option is that this part of the bill be not commenced with,

and that we start a, as I said, a considered and careful process with a great degree of public consultation as well, of course, on the sort of intelligence collection regime that will be appropriate for a police force going forward in the modern era with all the digital innovation available to us.

Thank you. Thank you very much. I've got Todd Stevenson. We've got a number of questions lining up for him. Thank you. Thank you, Commissioner, and your team for your detailed submission. Great work. Couple of things I just want to explore. One is, I would certainly can't speak for the whole committee, but I'd certainly be interested in your additional safeguards that you would like to see put in place. I think one of the issues...

And everything you've raised, super important. I think we, yeah, there might be some things we can do to this bill, one, to address some of the issues you have done, and then I really am interested in this idea around a framework for police intelligence gathering. Do you think there's a way we could strengthen the bill by putting in some safeguards, but then also noting in, say, regulations that there has to be this framework that you're talking about?

Could you see some way we could achieve what you want, but not ne- well, delay maybe implementation, but at least have it available to be done in the future? Sure. Through you, Mr. Chair. I guess if I think about safeguards, I think about four areas. First, tighter thresholds for police collection of information, needing to assess whether it's necessary to collect the information, whether the privacy benefits of the collection and proposed use are proportionate to the expected benefits for policing functions.

So tighter thresholds reflecting the Privacy Act principles there. Second, controls on the storage, retention and destruction, and secondary use of that information collected under the powers in the bill. Third, accountability and transparency provisions, such as requirements for record keeping, auditing, public recording, and publication of policies relating to the exercise of the police's powers.

And fourth, of course, oversight of police compliance with safeguards by an independent body. One of our concerns, of course, with the provisions as drafted in part one, is that they displace the Privacy Act, and therefore the ability of my office to exercise some prudential oversight of what the police is doing in this space. In terms of primary legislation or regulation, I guess first, it would be useful to have, even if it's at a higher level, such safeguards specified in legislation, and then with the ability for, for example, for the responsible minister to

make regulations to provide some more detail. We would expect that regulations-making power to not be discretionary, that it would be a requirement of the minister to make those regulations through the correct process with public consultation, of course. If I think about other-Agencies that are active in this space, in the surveillance space.

We have, of course, two security and intelligence agencies which carry out their intrusive functions within a very sophisticated and comprehensive network of statutory provisions and oversight of what they do. And one of my concerns, of course, is that in the area of surveillance, we have to, as a nation, be careful to avoid what I would call surveillance creep by other state agencies.

We have two agencies who, for special and particular reasons, have extraordinary surveillance powers, and those are undertaken within a, as I say, comprehensive statutory framework to protect the rights and freedoms of New Zealand citizens. I would be very wary of similar powers being given to other state agencies without such a similar set of safeguards being in place.

All right. Maybe further to Todd's question, I think it'd be useful if you could supply some further thoughts on it. Just on the oversight, you also suggest in your report the appointment or oversight by the new Inspector General of Police, and that's one recommendation. Earlier, we heard about the general data protection regulation that many international countries adopt. Can you provide a view on whether we need both?

One, and are you clear that the Inspector General is the right organization to do any oversight if we were to impose something? Sure. My colleagues might wish to say more on that, but what I would say is that we have seen developments in other countries on the other side of the world and closer to home in Australia, where the oversight bodies that look after, for example, the intelligence agencies as I've just been talking about, have actually had their authority extended to cover the police carrying out such surveillance activities.

But we, of course, have had the proposal to have an Inspector General of Police. I think at the end of the day, it would come to how the responsibilities, roles, and powers of that agency are drawn up. I would see it as a place where there could be a number of oversight bodies. I would hope that the Office of the Privacy Commissioner would remain one of those with oversight responsibilities, as well as an Inspector General of Police. Anything further you want to say on that, Joanne?

I think just to add to that, I think the important priority is to make sure this bill anticipates oversight, so that it includes the safeguards that mean that any oversight body can engage with police activities. Unfortunately, the Inspector General of Police has not yet come to fruition. But if the bill is established in such a way that anticipates oversight, then you can deal with the choice of oversight body perhaps further down the track once that Inspector General comes on stream.

So you're not specifically proposing Inspector General, but you're saying that that is one possibility? Or are you saying when it comes to surveillance, and we've got intelligence, we've got other activities, we should be adopting what we have for these GCIs or SIS type arrangement? Have you got a clearer view on that?

I guess what, just to clarify for the committee, Mr. Chair, what I'm saying is if we went through, for example, a different process than the one before the committee today, where there was a considered and careful process to develop a bespoke standalone legislative framework for police surveillance activities, I would absolutely see a role for a future Inspector General of Police in relation to that.

What we'd need to be sure about, though, is that we didn't have a situation where the police had commenced, for example, carrying out a new regime of surveillance activity without that oversight being in place. Right. Okay. Get the point. You talked about the objective for holding data and being very clear about it, and you're saying the bill doesn't.

Section 45 talks about any other lawful purpose. And so have you got clear proposals around how you'd tighten that aspect? Ian, do you want to pitch with this? We don't have specific wording, but we do think that there needs to be a clear threshold and justification, a necessity test, and some sort of test of proportionality of the collection of information by police. So at the moment, the power that's in Section 45A, the other sections that would be introduced by the bill, are very

broad-ranging. And in particular, we're concerned that the requirement under the Privacy Act that you need to determine whether the collection of information is necessary would effectively be removed by this bill. So we would like that kind of threshold to be reintroduced in some way.

Do you think that was intentional to remove that, or do you think that's just unintentional consequence in terms of reference to the Privacy Commission? Well, the materials relating to this bill do indicate a policy intent for the Privacy Act to still apply.

Our analysis is that it will be displaced in some respects. So it is perhaps possible that it's been a misunderstanding or an unintentional consequence. But we do think that it would be the legal effect of this bill that, in particular, that first principle in the Privacy Act, which is central to the whole act, would be effectively displaced.

Okay, so it would be useful to get your thoughts on that, around maintaining that aspect. The one that no one sort of picked up on, but you do raise in your report, is about police employees. It's something that we've asked the officials about, but the understanding around that is where you have a crime scene and you've got people you might want to bring in specialists. What's your view on that?

Or did you simply not understand what that term meant? Yeah, we were simply asking the question, I guess, as to whether there was a rationale behind that, because some parts of the Policing Act, the powers in those act parts are restricted to constables.

Others are more general to police employees. So we were just wanting to test whether there was a good rationale, given that constables have to go through additional training and requirements in order to meet the test to become a constable. So it's good to hear that the committee has asked officials for advice on that point.

You made the comment about intelligence and intelligence purpose need to be better defined. Do you have a clear view around the difference between intelligence, intelligence purpose and surveillance, which relates to the act or, sorry, the bill?

Yes, Mr. Chair. That is why we raised the question. A lot could be packed into that word. The police do have existing guidance documents that refer to their intelligence function. And so as part of our discussions prior to the bill being introduced, we raised the question about the usefulness of having a definition of intelligence in the bill.

The clearer the bill is, the clearer New Zealanders will be aware of their rights and privileges in relation to it. And a lack of a definition in that space does create more uncertainty, we believe, and for not just members of the public, but also for police officers in carrying out their job.

And would you link that to a common definition with what the Secret Service agencies do, or do you think that police have their own form of intelligence gathering that's sort of a different intent? Obviously, you have different purpose. One is international or different reason for it. Do you have a view on that?

That would be something that the police would need to work through themselves for their own purposes, I think, and work up some proposals for potential legislative amendment to the bill. Okay, one more question and then I'll go to Camilla. We've also heard in submissions earlier today around the need or the, sorry, the right to have videos or whatever information that's been collected maybe under surveillance to be deleted.

There doesn't seem to be any rules. Perhaps it's going to be covered in regulation. Do you have a view around whether there should be a mandatory requirement, or you have a right to be able to see what's on file? Obviously, that then becomes subject to criminals actually trying to stop stuff being held. But how would you see the balance of that right to delete material that isn't relevant versus the ability of keeping New Zealanders safe?

Well, Mr. Chair, under the current Privacy Act provisions, everybody has, through information privacy principle nine, the ability to ask why information is being retained. There is a retention provision which says that information shouldn't be kept for longer than it's needed or there's a reasonable reason to keep it.

I think that one of the challenges with the provisions in part one is that, as I say, it displaces the effects of the Privacy Act. It also displaces that principle. There are good reasons not to retain information you don't need. One, obviously, the rights of New Zealanders. Second, the more information you retain, the costlier it is to keep that information. And third, the more information you hold, as we've seen with a number of privacy breaches in both the public and private sector, the more risk there is that that information could be taken in a cyber attack.

So there is strong public policy reasons for having a retention and erasure principle being applying to information collected in this space. Chair, is there anything more you want to say on rights to erasure in that space? Just to, yeah, affirm what you're saying, Commissioner, that the right to deletion and the principle about accurate and up-to-date information are important data protection principles.

So while the framework needs to enable police to retain what it needs to retain, the framework also needs to have flexibility to discard the information, particularly under a very broad authorization. There needs to be an ability to discard the information that police do not need to retain, so that the intelligence being retained is useful and fruitful intelligence, not simply scooping up a lot of information that turns out not in fact to be particularly useful and is information about New Zealand citizens and visitors.

Okay, cool. Right. Camilla? Thank you, and thank you very much for your substantive submission and for your discussion with the committee. I just have two questions and just wanting to really understand your submission to us. The first is, is it your submission that there needs to be a law change following the Tamifuna case? And the second question is, you've talked about the risk of possible legal challenges if the law is to proceed as it is currently outlined in this bill.

Is it your view that- That there is an increased risk with this legislation, or that simply it remains unclear because of the lack of detail which has been traversed a bit already today. Thank you. Thank you. I'll start with the second first, the risk of possible legal challenges.

I think I said in my opening remarks that you can't very easily legislate away the power of the court system. That is the purpose of the court system, after all, to interpret the law in relation to certain fact situations. Jo, is there anything more you want to say on that particular point? I think that our submission reflects some of the underlying messages from the joint OPC IPCA report and the Tamifuna decision, which is to say that the bill really only deals with half the problem.

It deals with the point of collection of information by police. But the messages from the joint report and from Tamifuna were really saying that you have to consider intelligence gathering within the full life cycle of the use of that information and its retention. And it's in this increasingly intensified technological progress, where that information is going into network databases for analysis and insight, and so on.

So really, the message from the Tamifuna court, and if you think about what His Honor Justice Koch was saying, he was really inviting Parliament to create a clear framework, not just about the authorization for police, but about retention, use, and destruction of information.

And so our perspective is that without addressing that side of the equation, which is useful for the system, it's useful for police to provide greater certainty. Without that part of the equation, the safeguards, there's likely still to be scope for information gathering to be challenged within the courts, and for the courts to have a look at that under the privacy settings which are embedded legally within the Privacy Act, the Search and Surveillance Act, and the New Zealand Bill of Rights Act.

All right. Hey, we have to bring it to a close. But thank you for your submission, a weighty submission. There's obviously Todd asked some specific stuff, and I've asked for some other stuff. We'd appreciate if you were able to help the committees do deliberations around those aspects. So if you could come back to us, that'd be very helpful.

But I want to thank you all for coming through this morning and giving your thoughts. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Thank you, members. Yes, if there are any further questions, very happy to respond to them through your advisors to the committee. And at least we haven't covered everything that was asked by Ms. Bellich.
Okay. All right, thank you. Now, members, we're going to go into private session just briefly, if you want to transfer. Thank you, everyone.
